original_hypothesis.title: buyShareCredFor Enables Continuous Sell-Lock Griefing on Any Curator
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: buyShareCredFor allows anyone to buy shares credited to any curator address. This sets lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp, resetting the 10-minute sell lock period for the target curator. An attacker can repeatedly call buyShareCredFor with amount=1 for a victim's address, continuously preventing them from selling their shares. The cost is only the price of 1 share at current supply.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The lock period is applied to the curator_ parameter rather than msg.sender. This creates an economic griefing vector where an attacker can lock a victim's position during unfavorable price movements, causing the victim to suffer losses from inability to exit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp — sets lock period for curator_ not for msg.sender. buyShareCredFor passes user-supplied curator_ address

preconditions: Victim holds cred shares they wish to sell
Attacker has enough ETH to buy 1 share repeatedly (bonding curve minimum price)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Cost per grief = price of 1 share at current supply + protocol fee + creator fee. At low supply ~0.001 ETH per grief.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call buyShareCredFor(credId, 1, victim, 0) every ~9 minutes to keep victim permanently locked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not directly profitable but enables: 1) Forcing losses during price drops 2) Competitive suppression 3) Extortion

economic_questions: What is the minimum cost per lock refresh at various supply levels?
Can batchBuyShareCred lock multiple creds for a victim simultaneously?
Is the attacker's share purchase itself recoverable after lock period?
validation_requirements: Calculate 1-share buy price at various supply levels
Verify batchBuyShareCred also resets lock for curator_ parameter
Test continuous lock griefing scenario over multiple blocks

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.001 ETH per lock refresh at low supply
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.001 ETH gas per transaction on L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect profit from forcing victim to hold during price decline
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on victim's position size and price movement

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During price decline or when victim signals intent to sell

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Unable to sell shares; losses from price decline during lock
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Victim's entire position in targeted cred

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm lock resets via buyShareCredFor with amount=1

validation_result.impact_summary: Anyone can continuously prevent any curator from selling their cred shares by repeatedly calling buyShareCredFor with amount=1, resetting the victim's 10-minute sell lock at minimal cost.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The buyShareCredFor function accepts an arbitrary curator_ address and passes it directly to _handleTrade. Inside _handleTrade's buy branch, lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] is set to block.timestamp. This timestamp is later checked in the sell branch: if block.timestamp <= lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] + SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD, the sell reverts. Since anyone can call buyShareCredFor specifying any curator_ address, an attacker can repeatedly reset a victim's sell lock timer by buying 1 share credited to the victim every ~9 minutes. The cost per griefing transaction is only the bonding curve price of 1 share plus protocol/creator fees. The same vulnerability exists in batchBuyShareCred which also accepts a curator_ parameter and sets lastTradeTimestamp for that curator in _executeBatchTrade.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Line references in _handleTrade confirm that lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp is set for the curator_ parameter (the share recipient), not for msg.sender (the buyer). The sell path enforces the lock period against the same curator_ address. buyShareCredFor is a public payable function with no restriction on who can specify which curator_ address.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Accepts arbitrary curator_ address from any caller. The curator_ is passed to _handleTrade where it will be used to set lastTradeTimestamp for that address, not for msg.sender.

The sell path checks lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] for the lock period. The buy path sets lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp for the same curator_ parameter. When buyShareCredFor is called with an attacker-chosen curator_, the lock is reset for the victim (curator_), not the actual buyer (msg.sender).

Same issue in batch path. batchBuyShareCred accepts curator_ parameter and _executeBatchTrade sets lastTradeTimestamp[credId][curator] for the supplied curator address, allowing lock reset across multiple credIds in a single transaction.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls buyShareCredFor(credId, 1, victimAddress, 0) with sufficient msg.value
2. buyShareCredFor passes curator_=victimAddress to _handleTrade(credId, 1, true, victimAddress, 0)
3. _handleTrade validates cred exists, checks supply < MAX_SUPPLY, verifies msg.value covers price+fees
4. _updateCuratorShareBalance credits 1 share to victimAddress in shareBalance[credId]
5. In isBuy branch: lastTradeTimestamp[credId][victimAddress] = block.timestamp — LOCK RESET
6. When victim calls sellShareCred(credId, amount, 0), _handleTrade checks block.timestamp <= lastTradeTimestamp[credId][victim] + 10 minutes
7. Since attacker just reset the timestamp, the sell reverts with ShareLockPeriodNotPassed
8. Attacker repeats every ~9 minutes to maintain continuous lock
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: buyShareCredFor → _handleTrade (isBuy=true) → lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp. The curator_ is the attacker-specified victim address. The sell path in _handleTrade checks this same timestamp: if (block.timestamp <= lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] + SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD) revert. Since the attacker controls when the buy happens and the lock is set on the victim's address, the attacker can keep the victim permanently locked out of selling.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No config dependency. SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD is hardcoded as 10 minutes. The vulnerability exists regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and highly realistic: (1) Victim holds shares in credId X. (2) Attacker calls buyShareCredFor(X, 1, victimAddress, 0) with enough ETH to cover 1 share. (3) This sets lastTradeTimestamp[X][victimAddress] = block.timestamp. (4) Victim cannot sell for 10 minutes. (5) Attacker repeats before 10 minutes elapse. The cost is the bonding curve price of 1 share (which increases with supply but remains relatively low) plus protocol and creator fees. On L2 chains, gas costs are negligible. The attacker also acquires the shares purchased, which have some residual value.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any curator can be indefinitely prevented from selling their shares. This is particularly damaging during price declines (e.g., when many users are selling, driving supply and price down), as the victim is forced to hold a depreciating position. The attack can also be used for competitive suppression, extortion, or to prevent a creator from selling. Using batchBuyShareCred, an attacker can lock a victim across multiple creds simultaneously in a single transaction.
recommended_mitigations: Set lastTradeTimestamp for msg.sender instead of curator_ when buying: change `lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp` to `lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][_msgSender()] = block.timestamp` in the buy path, and only check lock period for msg.sender during sell

Alternatively, track lock period per (credId, address) pair only when the address itself initiates the buy (i.e., in buyShareCred set lock for msg.sender; in buyShareCredFor do not set any lock for curator_)
Consider separate lock tracking: lastBuyTimestamp tracks when an address last initiated a buy for their own account, and the sell lock checks only this self-initiated buy timestamp
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Cred contract with bonding curve. 2. Create a cred (credId=1). 3. Have victim buy shares. 4. Warp forward 10+ minutes so victim can sell. 5. From attacker account, call buyShareCredFor(1, 1, victimAddress, 0) with sufficient ETH. 6. Immediately have victim attempt sellShareCred(1, amount, 0). 7. Verify it reverts with ShareLockPeriodNotPassed. 8. Warp 9 minutes, repeat attacker buy, verify victim still cannot sell.
uncertainties: The exact cost per grief depends on current supply level and bonding curve parameters, but at any supply level the minimum purchase of 1 share is feasible
The attacker accumulates shares that have some value, partially offsetting the griefing cost (though selling them also requires waiting the lock period)
additional_testing_needed: Calculate exact 1-share costs at various supply levels to quantify sustained attack cost
Test batchBuyShareCred with curator_ parameter to confirm multi-cred simultaneous lock
Verify whether the attacker can recover cost by selling the accumulated 1-share purchases after the lock period
